questions.question: Why do homogeneous coordinates use w=1 for points and w=0 for vectors?
options: w=1 makes translation affect the point (the 4th column of the matrix adds tx,ty,tz); w=0 zeroes out that column, so vectors are untouched by translation
w=0 is for points so they get divided by zero and become infinite; w=1 keeps vectors finite
The w component stores color alpha; points need alpha=1 and vectors need alpha=0 for blending
It is an arbitrary convention — swapping w=0 and w=1 produces identical results
questions.explanation: In a 4×4 translation matrix, the (0,3), (1,3), (2,3) entries hold tx, ty, tz. When multiplied by a column vector with w=1, the fourth row picks up the translation. With w=0, those terms vanish, correctly preserving direction vectors under translation.

questions.question: You compose transforms as T * R * S * v (column-vector convention). In what order are the transforms applied to the vertex v?
options: Scale, then Rotate, then Translate
Translate, then Rotate, then Scale
They are applied simultaneously — order does not matter
Only the last matrix (T) is applied because matrix multiplication overwrites previous transforms
questions.explanation: With column vectors, multiplication is right-to-left: v is first scaled by S, then the result is rotated by R, then translated by T. This is why the 'last' matrix in the expression acts 'first' on the vertex.

questions.question: A point at (1, 0, 0) is first translated by (5, 0, 0) then rotated 90° around Z. What is the result? What changes if you reverse the order?
options: TR gives (5, 1, 0); RT gives (0, 6, 0) — different results because rotation around the origin followed by translation moves the whole rotated shape, whereas translation followed by rotation orbits the point around the origin at the translated distance
TR and RT always give the same result because matrix multiplication is associative
TR gives (0, 6, 0); RT gives (5, 1, 0) — the results are simply swapped
Both give (5, 1, 0) because translation is commutative with rotation
questions.explanation: TR means: first Rotate (the point at (1,0,0) rotates to (0,1,0)), then Translate (+5 in x → (5,1,0)). RT means: first Translate (point moves to (6,0,0)), then Rotate (orbit (6,0,0) around origin by 90° → (0,6,0)). The order fundamentally changes the outcome.

questions.question: What does the perspective divide do, and why is it necessary?
options: It divides clip-space (x,y,z) by w to produce NDC, making objects farther from the camera appear smaller because w is proportional to eye-space depth
It divides by the frame rate to produce a smooth animation; without it objects jump between frames
It converts integers to floating point so the GPU can process them; it has nothing to do with depth
It normalizes the color channels by dividing RGB by w to fit in [0, 255]
questions.explanation: The projection matrix sets w_clip = -z_eye. After the matrix multiply, dividing x_clip/w_clip and y_clip/w_clip produces the foreshortening effect: as z increases (farther away), w increases, and x/w and y/w shrink. This is the algebraic heart of perspective projection.

questions.question: What is Gimbal lock and how do quaternions address it?
options: Gimbal lock occurs when two rotation axes align (e.g., pitch = ±90°), causing a loss of one degree of freedom; quaternions represent rotations in 4D and interpolate along the shortest arc, avoiding the axis-alignment problem entirely
Gimbal lock is a GPU rendering bug where triangles with the same normal disappear; quaternions fix it by doubling the vertex count
Gimbal lock happens when the w component reaches 0, making the perspective divide fail; quaternions prevent this by ensuring w is always 1
Gimbal lock is when the camera clips through a wall; quaternions solve this by adding collision detection
questions.explanation: Euler angles represent orientation as three sequential rotations. When the middle angle hits ±90°, two of the three axes collapse into one, and you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions (4D unit vectors) parameterize the space of rotations without any singularities, allowing smooth interpolation (SLERP) and composition without ever encountering axis alignment.

questions.question: Which coordinate system convention does OpenGL use by default, and what practical issue arises if you import a model authored in a different convention?
options: OpenGL uses a right-handed, Y-up coordinate system with Z pointing toward the viewer; importing a left-handed Z-into-screen model (DirectX/Unity convention) without conversion flips normals and inverts the depth test
OpenGL uses a left-handed Z-up system; all models are automatically converted by the driver so no issue arises
OpenGL uses a right-handed Z-up system; importing a Y-up model just changes the lighting brightness without affecting geometry
Coordinate system conventions have no effect on rendering — they only matter for physics engines
questions.explanation: A right-handed Y-up system (OpenGL default) has +Z toward the viewer. A left-handed system (DirectX default) has +Z into the screen. If you feed a left-handed model into a right-handed pipeline without flipping Z, the winding order of triangles reverses, normals point inward, and the depth buffer comparison is inverted. Always check and convert conventions at import time.
